old_claim.note: Lab-on-a-Chip provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency remains because point-of-care testing is defined by near-patient testing and rapid turnaround; lab-on-a-chip is a later miniaturized implementation path, not a prerequisite for the broader modality.

source_shape.source_locator: StatPearls describes point-of-care testing as testing at or near the site of patient care, with near-patient testing in the 1950s and the term point-of-care appearing in the early 1980s.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source defines POCT broadly through care location and rapid clinical turnaround rather than lab-on-a-chip microfluidic hardware.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Keeping lab-on-a-chip as a direct prerequisite would make a later microfluidic implementation necessary for older test-strip, lateral-flow, and immunoassay POCT workflows.

ontology_before: Point-of-care testing depended directly on lab-on-a-chip, making microfluidic chip miniaturization part of the general POCT definition.
ontology_after: Point-of-care testing depends on immunoassay diagnostics and can later be enhanced by lab-on-a-chip systems without requiring them.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: lab_on_a_chip is no longer a direct prerequisite.
would_reject_if: The node is later narrowed to microfluidic point-of-care devices rather than point-of-care testing generally.
A source shows the modeled historical POCT claim cannot operate without lab-on-a-chip hardware.
A dependent requires point_of_care_testing to imply microfluidic chip integration.
short_reason: Lab-on-a-chip is a later POCT implementation path, not a direct prerequisite for POCT itself.
